questions.question: Using Newton-Raphson method to find the square root of 2, starting with x₀ = 1.5, the value of x₁ is
The order of convergence of Newton-Raphson method is
questions.explanation: Newton-Raphson method has quadratic (order 2) convergence near the root, meaning the error approximately squares each iteration.

questions.question: Simpson's 1/3 rule requires the number of intervals to be
questions.explanation: Simpson's 1/3 rule applies to pairs of intervals (parabolic approximation), so the total number of intervals must be even.

questions.question: For solving the system Ax = b using Gauss-Seidel, convergence is guaranteed if matrix A is
questions.explanation: Gauss-Seidel is guaranteed to converge if A is strictly diagonally dominant (|aᵢᵢ| > Σⱼ≠ᵢ |aᵢⱼ| for all i). Symmetric positive definite also works but is a narrower condition.

questions.question: The error in trapezoidal rule integration is proportional to
questions.explanation: The global truncation error of the trapezoidal rule is O(h²), where h is the step size. This is derived from the second-order Taylor expansion of the integrand.

questions.question: In the bisection method applied to f(x) = x³ - x - 1 on [1, 2], after the first iteration the new interval is
questions.explanation: Midpoint = (1+2)/2 = 1.5. Evaluate: f(1) = 1-1-1 = -1 < 0, f(1.5) = 3.375-1.5-1 = 0.875 > 0. Since f(1)<0 and f(1.5)>0, the sign change (and hence the root) lies in [1, 1.5]. The new bisection interval is [1, 1.5].

questions.question: The Newton-Raphson iteration formula for finding the reciprocal of a number N (i.e., 1/N) without division is
questions.explanation: To find 1/N, set f(x) = 1/x - N = 0. Then f'(x) = -1/x². NR iteration: xₙ₊₁ = xₙ - (1/xₙ - N)/(-1/xₙ²) = xₙ + xₙ²(1/xₙ - N) = xₙ + xₙ - Nxₙ² = xₙ(2 - Nxₙ). This avoids division.

questions.question: Using Lagrange interpolation with points (0, 1), (1, 3), (2, 7), the interpolating polynomial at x = 1.5 gives
Simpson's 3/8 rule requires the number of subintervals to be a multiple of
questions.explanation: Simpson's 3/8 rule uses cubic polynomials fitted over 3 consecutive subintervals at a time. Therefore the total number of subintervals must be a multiple of 3. The weights follow the pattern 1, 3, 3, 1 across each group of 3 intervals.

questions.question: Using Euler's method with h = 0.1 to solve dy/dx = x + y, y(0) = 1, the value y(0.1) is
questions.explanation: Euler's method: yₙ₊₁ = yₙ + h·f(xₙ, yₙ). At x₀=0, y₀=1: f(0,1) = 0+1 = 1. y(0.1) = 1 + 0.1×1 = 1.1. Note: exact solution is (2eˣ - x - 1), giving y(0.1)≈1.1103, but Euler gives 1.1.

questions.question: The 4th-order Runge-Kutta method (RK4) makes how many function evaluations per step?
questions.explanation: RK4 computes k₁ = hf(xₙ, yₙ), k₂ = hf(xₙ+h/2, yₙ+k₁/2), k₃ = hf(xₙ+h/2, yₙ+k₂/2), k₄ = hf(xₙ+h, yₙ+k₃). That's 4 function evaluations per step. The final update is yₙ₊₁ = yₙ + (k₁+2k₂+2k₃+k₄)/6.

questions.question: The order of error in Simpson's 1/3 rule is
questions.explanation: The global truncation error of Simpson's 1/3 rule is O(h⁴), i.e., proportional to h⁴. This is why it is more accurate than the trapezoidal rule (O(h²)). The local error is O(h⁵) per panel.

questions.question: In Gauss-Seidel iteration, compared to Jacobi iteration, the method

options.A: Uses updated values immediately within each iteration
options.B: Uses only values from the previous iteration
options.D: Always converges faster for any matrix

questions.explanation: In Gauss-Seidel, as soon as a new value xᵢ is computed, it is immediately used to compute xᵢ₊₁ in the SAME iteration. Jacobi uses only old values from the previous iteration. This makes Gauss-Seidel generally (but not always) faster to converge.

questions.question: Newton's forward difference interpolation formula is best suited for interpolation

options.A: Near the beginning of a tabulated data set
options.B: Near the end of a tabulated data set
options.C: At the midpoint of a tabulated data set

questions.explanation: Newton's forward difference formula uses differences Δy₀, Δ²y₀, ... built from the first point. It is most accurate when interpolating near the start of the table. Newton's backward difference formula is used near the end. Stirling's/Bessel's formulae work near the middle.

questions.question: Which of the following methods has linear (first-order) convergence for root-finding?
questions.explanation: Bisection method converges linearly (order 1) — each iteration reduces the interval by exactly half, giving constant error reduction ratio of 0.5. Newton-Raphson: order 2 (quadratic). Secant: order ≈1.618 (superlinear). Halley's: order 3 (cubic).
